filled.diagnosis_and_prognosis: The claimant sustained soft tissue injuries to the cervical spine (whiplash), bilateral shoulders, and the lumbar spine as a result of the index accident. He also sustained bilateral elbow bruising, transient paraesthesia predominantly affecting the right hand, and transient lightheadedness and dizziness. The cervical spine and shoulder symptoms were severe in the immediate aftermath, progressing from severe at their peak to mild at the date of examination. The lumbar spine symptoms remain moderate and are ongoing, with significant sleep disturbance. The elbow bruising and paraesthesia resolved within approximately two weeks of the accident. The claimant also reports travel anxiety and hypervigilance whilst driving, which he states he is managing without formal psychological intervention at present. With regard to prognosis, the cervical spine and shoulder injuries are expected to continue to improve and are approaching resolution. The lumbar spine injury is expected to improve gradually, with approximately fifty percent improvement anticipated within six months of the date of injury and further improvement over the following six months. However, given the physical nature of the claimant's occupation as a carpenter, it is anticipated that ten to twenty percent of residual lumbar symptoms may persist beyond twelve months from the date of injury. A minimum of three months of further physiotherapy and rehabilitation is recommended from the date of examination. If the lumbar spine symptoms fail to improve, an MRI scan of the lumbar spine may be indicated to exclude structural pathology. The psychological symptoms are expected to resolve in due course without formal intervention, though this will be kept under review.
filled.document_review_notes: The claimant reported that X-rays of the cervical spine and thoracolumbar spine were performed at John Radcliffe Hospital on 06/12/2025, along with an MRI scan of the spine. The treating doctor initially suspected a chip fracture in the back on X-ray imaging; however, the subsequent MRI scan did not confirm this finding. The consulting doctor noted the intention to review the imaging records provided to ascertain whether the MRI scan included coverage of the lumbar spine, and to determine whether further imaging of the lumbar spine is required.
filled.effect_on_life: The claimant reports significant disruption to his daily life and recreational activities as a consequence of the index accident. In the initial period following the accident, the claimant was unable to perform basic tasks such as putting on socks and trousers due to severe pain and restriction of movement in the lumbar spine. He was required to sleep downstairs for approximately three weeks owing to significant sleep disturbance caused by his lower back pain, which was also disturbing his partner's sleep. The claimant was unable to participate in his hobbies of football and running for approximately two months following the accident. He attempted to return to football in January 2026 but was unable to continue due to pain. The claimant reports that he has been unable to play with his children as he would wish, particularly his eight-year-old son, and this limitation remains ongoing at the date of examination. He was unable to drive for approximately one week following the accident. Sleep disturbance remains a significant ongoing issue, with the claimant waking every thirty to forty-five minutes due to lower back pain and being required to frequently change position throughout the night.
filled.effect_on_work: The claimant is employed as a carpenter. He was absent from work for two days immediately following the accident due to an inability to move his neck and the consequent inability to drive safely. Upon his return, the claimant was placed on light duties for approximately one month, during which period he was collected from home by colleagues as he was unable to drive his work van. He thereafter undertook a gradual phased return to his full duties. At the date of examination, the claimant has returned to full duties; however, he reports that his lower back pain is exacerbated by physically demanding days at work, noting noticeable aching when he has been particularly active with bending and lifting.
examination_findings: Cervical spine: Full range of movement observed on flexion, extension, left lateral rotation, and right lateral rotation. The claimant reported a pulling sensation and sharp pain radiating into the right shoulder region and below the right ear on flexion. No tenderness elicited on palpation of the posterior cervical spine.

Shoulders: Full range of movement observed bilaterally with no restriction. Tenderness elicited on palpation of the trapezius muscles bilaterally, worse on the right side than the left. Periscapular tenderness noted on the right.

Lumbar spine: Forward flexion reported as reduced, with the claimant describing an inability to bend forward to put on socks, requiring him to bring his foot up to his knee in a seated position. Rotation movements reported as achievable through full range but accompanied by pain in the midline lower back, just above the coccyx. No current neurological symptoms reported in the lower limbs at the date of examination.
filled.immediate_medical_attendance: The claimant did not seek immediate medical attention on the day of the accident on 05/12/2025. He experienced mild aching in the neck and shoulders that evening and took a hot bath. The following day, 06/12/2025, his symptoms worsened significantly, with a complete loss of movement in the neck and severe stiffness. By the evening of 06/12/2025, the claimant telephoned NHS 111. He was advised by an out-of-hours general practitioner to attend John Radcliffe Hospital. He attended the same-day unit at John Radcliffe Hospital, where X-rays of the cervical spine and back were performed, followed by an MRI scan of the spine. The MRI scan did not confirm a suspected chip fracture that had been queried on the X-ray. The claimant was prescribed codeine for pain relief and was diagnosed with whiplash injuries.
filled.incident_description: The claimant reports that on 05/12/2025, at approximately half past three in the afternoon, he was driving his work van on a road situated between two roundabouts. He had come off a roundabout onto a road that transitions from a dual carriageway into a single lane before widening again into two lanes approaching the next roundabout, which is controlled by traffic lights. It was school-run time and traffic had built up. The claimant was stationary in queuing traffic when he felt a massive impact to the rear of his van. The force of the collision pushed his van into the vehicle in front, which was a taxi, and displaced his van to the right, into approximately the middle of the two lanes. The driver's airbag deployed and the cab filled with smoke. The claimant's eight-year-old son was a passenger in the vehicle. The claimant believes the third-party vehicle approached from behind and struck the rear right-hand side of his van at an angle, which would account for the deployment of the driver's airbag but not the passenger airbag. After exiting their vehicles, the claimant and the driver of the taxi observed the third-party vehicle slowly reversing before the driver accelerated and fled the scene. The claimant's van was written off, sustaining severe damage to the rear, with both rear doors caved in and the metalwork displaced over the rear tyre, as well as significant frontal damage from the secondary impact with the taxi. The claimant's partner attended the scene to collect his son.
filled.living_arrangements: The claimant lives with his partner and two children, aged eight and two.
filled.medical_history: The claimant reports no significant past medical history. He was fit and well prior to the index accident.
filled.photo_id_confirmation: The claimant's identity was verified by means of a driving licence, and the details were confirmed to match those held on file.
physical_symptoms: Neck pain: Onset on the day of the accident with mild aching, worsening to severe the following day with complete loss of movement. Currently mild with intermittent exacerbations, particularly on quick movements of the neck which produce a sharp pain radiating into the right shoulder.

Bilateral shoulder pain: Onset on the day of the accident with mild aching, worsening to severe the following day. Currently mild with residual stiffness and awareness of discomfort on movement. Right side worse than left.

Lower back pain: Onset the day after the accident, initially severe with significant restriction of forward flexion. Currently moderate and ongoing, with pain localised to the midline lower lumbar region just above the coccyx. The claimant reports difficulty bending forward to perform tasks such as putting on socks. Pain is exacerbated by physical activity and prolonged periods in any one position.
Sleep disturbance: Ongoing since the accident. The claimant wakes every thirty to forty-five minutes due to lower back pain and is required to change position frequently throughout the night.
Bilateral elbow bruising: Moderate severity at onset, believed to result from the claimant's elbows striking the seat on impact. Resolved within approximately two weeks.
Paraesthesia (tingling) in both hands, predominantly the right: Mild severity, onset within the first week following the accident. Resolved within approximately seven to ten days.
Lightheadedness and dizziness: Mild to moderate severity, onset within the first week following the accident. Resolved within approximately seven to ten days.
filled.pre_existing_conditions: The claimant reports no pre-existing medical conditions.
filled.psychological_symptoms: The claimant reports ongoing travel anxiety and hypervigilance whilst driving. He describes constantly checking his mirrors and being excessively aware of his surroundings. He reports feeling tense when stationary at roundabouts or in traffic and vehicles approach from behind, regardless of their speed. He also reports anxiety about his partner and children when they are travelling by car, even when he is not present in the vehicle. The claimant states that the psychological symptoms were more pronounced in the first two weeks following the accident and have improved to some extent since that time. He considers that he is managing these symptoms without formal psychological intervention at present.
filled.rehabilitative_treatment: The claimant underwent a course of eight sessions of physiotherapy, arranged through his insurer. The physiotherapy focused primarily on the lumbar spine, with the cervical and shoulder symptoms responding relatively quickly. The consulting doctor has recommended a further minimum of three months of physiotherapy and rehabilitation from the date of examination, including strengthening exercises and potentially hydrotherapy, to address the ongoing lumbar spine symptoms. An MRI scan of the lumbar spine may be requested if the existing imaging does not adequately cover the lumbar region or if symptoms fail to improve.
filled.subsequent_medical_attendance: The claimant attended John Radcliffe Hospital on 06/12/2025, the day after the accident, following a telephone consultation with an out-of-hours general practitioner via NHS 111. He underwent X-rays of the cervical spine and back and an MRI scan of the spine at the same-day unit. He was prescribed codeine for pain relief. Subsequently, the claimant underwent eight sessions of physiotherapy arranged through his insurer, which commenced shortly after the accident.
